cookbook.title: Gathered at the Table
cookbook.subtitle: A collection of cherished recipes
cookbook.description: These are the recipes that fill kitchens with warmth and tables with love. Simple, honest, and full of flavour — cooked with care and shared with joy.
cookbook.author: A family collection

categories.name: Soups
categories.description: Warming bowls to nourish the soul

categories.name: Sweet Treats
categories.description: A little sweetness goes a long way

recipes.title: Lentil Soup
recipes.description: Don't settle for a bland lentil soup. Make it right, and you'll have everyone begging for seconds — and thirds! The touch of spices and finishing it off with lemon really lifts this soup to the next level.
recipes.pullQuote: The touch of lemon at the end lifts everything.
recipes.chefNotes: Don't rush the onion and celery stage — this is the flavour base of the entire soup. A good 7–10 minutes here makes all the difference.
recipes.tips: Use green or brown lentils; they hold their shape better than red. If using red lentils, the soup will be silkier and thicker.
recipes.storage: Keeps in the fridge for up to 5 days. The flavour improves overnight.
recipes.freezing: Freezes beautifully for up to 3 months. Cool completely before freezing in portions.
recipes.variations: Try adding a swirl of yoghurt and a pinch of chilli flakes to serve. For a richer broth, use chicken stock instead of vegetable.

ingredients.item: dried lentils, green or brown, rinsed
vegetable or chicken stock, low sodium

method: Heat oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add garlic and onion, cook for 2 minutes.
Add celery and carrot. Cook for 7–10 minutes or until softened and the onion is sweet. Don't rush this step — it is key to the flavour base of the soup.
Add all remaining ingredients except the lemon and salt. Stir to combine.
Increase heat and bring to a simmer. Scoop any scum from the surface and discard. Place lid on and turn heat down to medium-low. Simmer for 35–40 minutes or until lentils are soft.
Remove bay leaves.

Using a stick blender, do 2 or 3 quick pulses to thicken the soup slightly — you want it chunky, not smooth. Alternatively, transfer 2 cups to a blender, cool slightly, blend, and stir back into the pot.
Taste and season with salt. Add lemon juice to taste. Serve topped with fresh parsley and a drizzle of olive oil, with warm bread alongside.

recipes.title: Red Lentil Soup with Lemon
recipes.description: A Melissa Clark classic from the New York Times — this is the lentil soup that makes you stop, taste, and savour. Vibrant with cumin, brightened with lemon, and finished with good olive oil.
recipes.pullQuote: The soup that makes you stop, taste, and truly savour.
recipes.chefNotes: Use the best olive oil you have for drizzling at the end — it makes a real difference to the final flavour.
recipes.tips: Red lentils break down and create a naturally creamy, silky texture without any cream needed. Purée only half the soup for a lovely chunky-smooth balance.
recipes.storage: Refrigerate for up to 4 days. Reheat gently, adding a splash of water if needed.
recipes.freezing: Freezes well for up to 3 months.
recipes.variations: Add a pinch of turmeric for a beautiful golden colour. Top with crispy fried onions for texture.

ingredients.item: olive oil, plus extra for drizzling
ground chilli powder or cayenne, more to taste

method: In a large pot, heat 3 tablespoons of oil over high heat until hot and shimmering. Add onion and garlic, and sauté until golden, about 4 minutes.
Stir in tomato paste, cumin, salt, black pepper and chilli powder or cayenne, and sauté for 2 minutes longer.
Add broth, 2 cups water, lentils and carrot. Bring to a simmer, then partially cover the pot and turn heat to medium-low. Simmer until lentils are soft, about 30 minutes.
Taste and add salt if necessary. Using an immersion or regular blender, purée half the soup then add it back to the pot. The soup should be somewhat chunky.
Reheat soup if necessary, then stir in lemon juice and cilantro. Serve drizzled with good olive oil and dusted lightly with chilli powder if desired.

recipes.title: Granola
recipes.description: A nourishing, deeply satisfying granola built on tahini, honey and warming spices. High in fibre, rich in good fats, and endlessly adaptable. Make a big batch and it will serve you all week.
recipes.pullQuote: Tahini and honey transform ordinary oats into something extraordinary.
recipes.chefNotes: Pressing the mixture tightly into the tray before baking is the secret to getting good clusters. Don't stir it too early — let it set.
recipes.tips: The granola will still feel soft when it comes out of the oven. It firms up completely as it cools, so resist the temptation to bake it longer.
recipes.storage: Store in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 3 weeks.
recipes.freezing: Can be frozen in portions for up to 3 months.
recipes.variations: Swap the honey for maple syrup to make it vegan. Add dried fruit such as cranberries or apricots after baking once cooled.
recipes.nutritionNote: Gut Health: High in fibre (porridge oats and nuts). Brain Fog: Thiamin B1 (oats, seeds), Vitamin E (nuts & seeds), Magnesium (nuts & seeds), Iron (nuts). Bone Health: Calcium (tahini).
method: Preheat your oven to 180°C (350°F).

In a large bowl, combine the olive oil, tahini, honey, and vanilla extract. Whisk until the mixture is smooth. Add the spices — cinnamon, nutmeg, cardamom and ginger — and whisk again until well combined.
In a separate bowl, weigh out and combine all the dry ingredients: oats, walnuts, cashews, almonds, chia seeds, sunflower seeds and pumpkin seeds.
Add the dry ingredients to the bowl with the wet ingredients. Mix until everything is well coated.
Pour the mixture onto a lined baking tray. Use a spoon or spatula to press it down tightly and evenly — this is key for good clusters.
Place the tray in the preheated oven. Bake for 40 minutes, turning it halfway through to ensure even cooking.
Once baked, allow to cool completely on the tray. The granola will crisp up as it cools. Transfer to an airtight container for storage.

recipes.title: Nut Roast
recipes.description: A proper nut roast — rich with chestnut mushrooms, mixed nuts, red lentils and mature cheddar, served with a simple rosemary tomato sauce. This is the centrepiece worth making.
recipes.pullQuote: The centrepiece worth making — rich, golden, and deeply satisfying.
recipes.chefNotes: Cook the lentil mixture until it is fairly dry before adding the remaining ingredients. Too much moisture and the loaf won't hold together when sliced.
recipes.tips: This is actually better made the day before. Cool completely, refrigerate, then slice and reheat — it holds its shape perfectly and the flavours deepen overnight.
recipes.storage: Keeps refrigerated for up to 4 days. Reheat slices in a pan or oven.
recipes.freezing: Freezes well — cool, slice, and freeze with parchment between slices for up to 3 months.
recipes.variations: To make vegan: use an extra tbsp of oil in place of butter, omit the cheese and use 3 tbsp egg replacer. Bake for 1 hour.

ingredients.item: red pepper, halved, deseeded and finely diced
mixed nuts (walnuts, pecans, hazelnuts, Brazil nuts), roughly chopped

method: Heat the oven to 180°C / fan 160°C / gas 4 and line the base and sides of a 1.5 litre loaf tin with parchment paper.
Heat 1 tbsp olive oil and 15g butter in a large frying pan and cook 1 finely chopped large onion and 2 finely chopped celery sticks for about 5 mins until beginning to soften.
Stir in 2 finely chopped garlic cloves and 200g finely chopped chestnut mushrooms and cook for a further 10 mins.
Stir in 1 finely diced red pepper and 1 grated carrot and cook for about 3 mins, then add 1 tsp dried oregano and 1 tsp smoked paprika and cook for just a minute.

Add 100g red lentils and 2 tbsp tomato purée and cook for about 1 min, then add 300ml vegetable stock and simmer over a very gentle heat until all the liquid has been absorbed and the mixture is fairly dry. This should take about 25 minutes. Set aside to cool.

Stir in 100g fresh breadcrumbs, 150g chopped mixed nuts, 3 lightly beaten large eggs, 100g grated mature cheddar, a handful of finely chopped flat-leaf parsley and a pinch of salt and some ground black pepper.
Stir to mix well then spoon the mixture into the prepared tin and press down the surface.
Cover with foil and bake for 30 mins, then remove the foil and bake for a further 20 mins until firm when pressed gently.
Meanwhile, to make the sauce, heat 2 tbsp extra virgin olive oil very gently then add 2 finely sliced garlic cloves and 1 rosemary sprig and heat without colouring.
Pour in 400ml passata and add a pinch of salt and some ground black pepper. Simmer gently for just 15 mins.
Allow the loaf to cool in the tin for about 10 mins then turn out onto a serving board or plate. Remove the baking paper and cut into slices and serve with a little of the tomato sauce.

recipes.title: Smokey Potatoes, Peppers & Chickpeas
recipes.description: A one-tray wonder inspired by Spanish flavours — paprika, garlic and lemon with potatoes, peppers, tomatoes and chickpeas. The perfect mid-week supper that practically cooks itself.
recipes.pullQuote: Just turn on the oven — let it do the work.
recipes.chefNotes: Roasted lemon is a revelation. It goes extra sweet and juicy in the oven, and squeezing it hot over everything transforms the dish.
recipes.tips: Reserve the aquafaba (chickpea liquid) from the tin — you can use it to make a fantastic vegan mayonnaise to serve alongside.
recipes.storage: Best eaten fresh from the oven. Leftovers keep for 2 days and reheat well.
recipes.freezing: Not recommended — the texture of the potatoes and chickpeas deteriorates.
recipes.variations: Try adding a handful of olives in the last 10 minutes of cooking. A sprinkle of smoked almonds to serve adds a lovely crunch.
method: Heat your oven to 200°C. Chop all the vegetables into bite-sized pieces and scatter into a baking tray.

Drain the chickpeas (reserve the aquafaba if you'd like to make mayonnaise or a vegan cake later) and add to the tray. Season with salt, pepper, smoked paprika and garlic, drizzle over the oil and mix well.
Add the lemon half, cut-side up, in the centre of the tray and roast for 20–30 minutes or until the vegetables are soft and starting to take on some colour.
Remove the tray from the oven and scatter over fresh parsley. Use tongs to squeeze the hot roasted lemon over everything. Enjoy with some salad leaves and mayonnaise.

recipes.title: Cheddar & Sage Scones
recipes.description: Make sure you use a punchy mature cheese to shine through these savoury scones made with mustard and buttermilk. Eat while still warm, spread with butter.
recipes.pullQuote: Eat while still warm, spread generously with butter.
recipes.chefNotes: The key to light scones is a light touch. Work the dough as little as possible — just until it comes together. Overworking develops the gluten and makes them tough.
recipes.tips: Use the coldest butter you have — straight from the fridge is ideal. Cold fat = flaky, well-risen scones.
recipes.storage: Best eaten on the day. Store in an airtight container for up to 2 days, or freeze.
recipes.freezing: Freeze baked scones individually, then reheat from frozen at 180°C for 10 minutes.
recipes.variations: Try with rosemary instead of sage, or add a pinch of cayenne to the flour for extra warmth.

ingredients.item: fresh sage, finely chopped, plus 8 small leaves

method: Heat oven to 220°C / 200°C fan / gas 7. Mix the flour, mustard powder, ½ tsp salt and a grinding of black pepper in a large bowl. Rub in the butter until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Stir in half the cheese and all the sage. Mix together the egg and buttermilk in a separate bowl.

Make a well in the centre of the flour mix and pour in all but ½ tbsp of the buttermilk mix. Working quickly, stir until the mixture forms a soft, spongy dough. Tip onto a lightly floured surface and knead briefly until smooth.

Roll out to a 3cm-thick square. Cut into quarters, then half each quarter diagonally, so you have 8 triangles. Place the scones on a floured baking tray, brush with the remaining buttermilk, sprinkle over the remaining cheese and top each with a sage leaf.
Bake for 12–14 mins until they are well risen, golden and sound hollow when tapped on the bottom. Eat while still warm, spread with butter.

recipes.title: Vanilla Coconut Cupcakes
recipes.description: Light, fragrant cupcakes with desiccated coconut, maple syrup and a hint of vanilla. Can be made with spelt flour or gluten-free with buckwheat and rice flour.
recipes.pullQuote: Strangely magical — they start so liquidy you think they won't work, then they get thick.
recipes.chefNotes: Once you combine the wet and dry bowls, let the batter stand for 2 minutes before spooning into cases. This is when the magic happens — you'll see it firm up right before your eyes.
recipes.tips: Mini cupcakes are ready in just 15 minutes. Check with a skewer — it should come out clean.
recipes.storage: Store in an airtight container for up to 3 days at room temperature.
recipes.freezing: Freeze unfrosted cupcakes for up to 3 months. Thaw at room temperature.
recipes.variations: If allergic to coconut, substitute with almond meal or crushed sunflower seeds. For a very low fructose version, use half coconut nectar and half rice syrup instead of maple syrup.

ingredients.item: organic white or wholemeal spelt flour (or 100g buckwheat + 90g rice flour for gluten free)

items.item: butter, ghee, or coconut oil, melted
natural vanilla extract or vanilla bean powder

method: Preheat the oven to 180°C and line a muffin tray with cupcake cases.
Combine all dry ingredients in one bowl: flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda and desiccated coconut. Mix well.
In a separate bowl, combine all the wet ingredients: melted butter or oil, apple cider vinegar, vanilla, maple syrup and rice milk or egg.

Combine the two bowls together with a wooden spoon, stirring gently until just combined — do not overmix. Let the batter stand for 2 minutes to firm up (it will transform from liquid to thick as you watch).
Spoon the batter into your lined muffin cases, filling each about two-thirds full.
Bake for 25–30 minutes, or about 15 minutes for mini cupcakes. Test with a skewer — it should come out clean. Cool on a rack before serving.

recipes.title: Raw Broccoli Salad
recipes.description: Fresh and crunchy broccoli laced with the tang of red onion, the sweetness of red apple and the crunch of walnuts, all united in a maple mustard dressing.
recipes.pullQuote: Better after a rest in the fridge — the dressing soaks in and everything mellows.
recipes.chefNotes: Use the stalks as well as the florets — they add great texture and nothing is wasted.
recipes.tips: This salad is far better after it has had time to chill and marinate in the fridge for at least 20 minutes.
recipes.storage: Keeps in the fridge for up to 2 days. The broccoli softens slightly which is lovely.
recipes.variations: Swap walnuts for toasted almonds or sunflower seeds. Try dried cranberries instead of apple for a sweeter note.

items.item: sea salt & freshly ground black pepper

method: Chop both the florets and stalk of the broccoli into small, even pieces. Finely dice the red onion, slice the apple into thin matchsticks and roughly chop the walnuts. Combine in a large bowl.
To make the dressing, whisk together the olive oil, apple cider vinegar, Dijon mustard, maple syrup, garlic and a generous sprinkle of sea salt and black pepper. Taste and adjust the seasoning.

Pour the dressing over the broccoli mixture and mix well to combine. This salad can be served straight away, but is far better after it has had time to chill and marinate in the fridge (20 minutes at least). Transfer to a serving bowl and finish with a crack of black pepper.
